speaker: interviewer
text: --in the United States of America. And today is September 6, 1995. You were talking about, uh, uh, dinner and that nobody got up from the table until after your father was finished. And, uh, in the evenings, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I told you, we, we went to do our homework in, in the [NON-ENGLISH] because it was, uh, better lighting. And it was more comfortable than do it at home. With the children being in the house, it was very rough, very hard. We live a comfortable life. But as I mentioned before, [NON-ENGLISH]. And we were very happy, till 1939. 1939, September 1, on a Friday, like, uh, this year it came out, the war with Germany broke out. German attacked Poland. On Wednesday, the 6th, as, as today, I went down to the River Vistula. And the other side of Krakow was Podgorze. And I saw already German troops being in Podgorze. And later in the afternoon-- that was early in the morning. Later in the afternoon, they came into Krakow. And a month later, Poland fell to the Germans. Of course, there were some people who ran away to the Russian side. But, uh, again, with seven children, it was very hard for us to run away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- but the, the first time that I saw brutality from the Germans was during September. We have the high holidays, Rosh Hashanah. And they went in [NON-ENGLISH] I normally liked very much cantors. I used to go to the Alta Shul, Cantor Goldberg; to the Kupa Shul, Cantor Kaufman. There was other cantors. There was a-- S-- S-- Stash-- er-- from Strasshof, uh, a good [NON-ENGLISH]. So I would like to hear them singing. So I would run out from my own synagogue, before my father started to pray in the [NON-ENGLISH]. And I would go to listen to the cantors. And sure enough on the street was German soldiers. I didn't recognize them before, the difference, but I saw it was Wehrmacht. It was absolutely not SS. They pulled out the Jews with the shuls and literally ripped their beards. They were shaving the beards and they were laughing, making fun. And they ripped the beards. And it made a terrible impact on me. But again, we were hoping that, uh, they're going to be defeated, because England and France already declared war. But I saw the brutality of the Germans. Later, in the-- every time came out new laws. First, we couldn't walk on the sidewalks. We had to walk on the street. Then they tell us that when you're over 13 years old, or 12, you had to wear a armband, a white armband with the Jewish star. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in December, when the first snow fell on Krakow, they circled around the Kazimierz, all the streets, because all where the Jewish people live. And they were screaming and yelling. And it, it strike me they were saying schmutzigen, that's 'dirty Jews.' Schmutzigen. Yet they went into the houses. And most people in my building were poor people. My mother had a little ring, a few silver candlesticks, no fur, no nothing. And they will take away everything. And they will put it together on the courtyard. As I told you, they put tables on the courtyard. And they will, right then and there, make packages and send away to their mothers or girlfriends, or-- and to me, they looked like a bunch of robbers. And they were so organized that for those people who they took away a lot of stuff, they gave them little pieces of paper that, uh, when the war ends, they will give it back. But they need the fur for the [NON-ENGLISH]. And they need this and they need that. They need for the war machine. But, uh, not only-- but to us, we were not hanged up on the material things. As long as they left us alone, we, we will be satisfied. In beginning of 1940, we were told that the large families have to move out from Krakow. They asked us to register, my father, my mother, my older brother, myself, and a younger brother. He was then 12 years old. The other ones, they didn't have to register. And voluntarily, as I have told you before, my father had a brother in Ozarow. So he went-- they say there's more food in a smaller town-- to leave for Ozarow. But we didn't want to leave the apartment with all our belongings we couldn't take along with us, so I stayed. I stayed to watch the apartment, so people would not go in and rob the apartment. There was another few boys from another apartment that they stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed together for a few months. And then it quieted down a little bit. So while it's quiet down, I wrote my parents they should come back. So they came back to Krakow. That was in the middle  the summer of 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1941, they start talking about making a ghetto. But to go into the ghetto, you had to have a small family, and you also had to have papers that you work for the German machinery, German Wehrmacht, or something, that you worked that the Germans have a use from it. Otherwise, you're going to be deported. And maybe the end of February or the first week of March in 1941, we sustain ourselves. We had some material, because money was no good at that time. We had shoes. We had pieces of goods. I would go with my brother, or sometimes I would go alone. On the other side of the Vistula was Podgorze, and farmers would come in. They would have a chicken, a few eggs, some other stuff, some food. And they, we will exchange. And I went one morning. I never wore the armband. I somehow got away with it, till one policeman, a Polish policeman, said to me, you must be Jewish. So I said to him-- and I spoke a perfect Polish. I even couldn't say the prayer for Mother Maria. And he insisted that, uh-- he, he took me to a corner, pulled my pants down, and  he saw that I am circumcised. And he took me to the Gestapo. I didn't want to tell them where I live, because I knew that at home there's, uh, my entire family. And I felt if the Gestapo going to shoot me, they're going to shoot me. That would be the end. There's nothing I can do about it. Somehow, one of the Gestapo said to the, to the people that there's a train with refugees from [? Milicz ?] going to Biala Podlaska. That's off the eastern, eastern border with the Russians. And the train is loaded with people, to put, uh, me on the train and take me there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was about six, seven days ride. I had nothing to eat. But the people on the train share with me. One gave me a piece of apple. One gave me a piece of bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came to Biala Podlaska. When I got to Biala Podlaska, the people were taken from the train into synagogues. And they would make partitions with, uh, covers. Every family had a little corner. And I had no family. I stayed by myself. So I asked the guy who was in charge, the judenrein, the Judenrat. And if he will take me home. So he had mercy on me. He took me home. I stayed there for a few days. He had a little store, a grocery. And I opened the store. And I wrote home that I'm going to leave Biala Podlaska and I'm coming home, back to Krakow. I got a return letter from my uncle-- from my father and mother not to come because they already received papers that they will also be shipped in the area of Biala Podlaska. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sure enough, it was between Purim-- I was Purim in Biala Podlaska-- between Purim and Pesach, was in middle of March or beginning of April, my entire family was shipped to Miedzyrzec. Miedzyrzec, [NON-ENGLISH] was 21 kilometer-- that was a 14, 15 miles-- from Biala Podlaska.

speaker: interviewer
text: One morning, I'm walking in the street in Biala Podlaska, I see my father. I said, Dad, what are you-- what are you doing here? He said, we all live on the-- I came to get you. And we're going-- I rented a home in Miedzyrzec. And we're going to be all together.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to Miedzyrzec. And in April, and May, and June, my older brother and myself went halfway from Miedzyrzec to Biala Podlaska to work in the forests. We worked for the Germans. We got paid. We worked for the Germans to cut the, the forest and to prepare it for an airport. And first we laid an airport. And at the end of the airport, you cut out the trees where a plane can go in and hide there. The forests there were very thick. And June 22, in 1941, the war broke out with the Russians. And while we were working there for three months, at night we were allowed to go home. We walked to work, the 10 kilometer, the 7 miles. And on the way back, we had a few dollars, a few zlotys. We will buy potatoes. And we could carry it on our shoulders and bring it home, or sometimes some eggs, whatever we could get from the farmers. And this how the family sustained itself. But  on June 22, when the war broke out, they said you cannot go home. You have to stay here. So we were thinking we're staying here for just   for two days, three days. In the meantime, they start making barbed wires. They make a camp. A day before they put the electric on the barbed wires, my brother and myself, the older brother, we ran away. We ran away, and we went to Miedzyrzec. We stayed at home maybe 10 minutes. We told them, don't worry about us. We're not going to stay in that camp. We're going on our way to half of Poland, to Lukow, and, and we are going back to Krakow. Somehow we're going to-- we had an uncle in Krakow, in the ghetto. And he said that some people are smuggling themself into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we decided we're going to go to the ghetto. And if they asked them, that they don't know, because we were worried, we were registered where we live.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we walked. We had one pair of shoes. So we carried on our shoulders, because we didn't want to ruin them. And we walked barefoot so we looked like shepherd boys. And at night, we never walked into a big city at night. We stayed at night-- some Poles would let us come in. And they would let us stay outdoor. They would give us some milk, some-- we would not take from them meat, because we wouldn't eat [NON-ENGLISH] some bread. And some will tell us, you can have bread and milk, but go away from my house. I don't want you to sleep near the house. I'm afraid of the Germans. But in all sincerity, I have no complaints. They had, uh, maybe mercy. Or they were kind and they help us out. One occasion, I stepped on a barbed wire where they keep the cows so they wouldn't run into the road. And I, I was bleeding. I couldn't stop the bleeding.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother used his urine, put it on me. And when we put the piece of cloth, and we were good again, till we came near Opole. In Opole, we had an aunt living there, so we want to make it to Opole.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we didn't want to stay there too long. But that was already going in in August, because it took us that long to walk. And we were afraid to go into-- they said that this area, the farmers are not so friendly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I, I run up a tree, and I cut green apples. The apples were not ripe yet. And I ate it, and I was very sick. But lucky, we were very close to Opole. And my aunt-- I was in bed. And she was in-- Opole had a ghetto. But they didn't bother the people. So-- Where they in the ghetto when you went there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto  in Opole.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were able to just walk right into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: I walked into the ghetto. They were not that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Strict.

speaker: interviewee
text: --strict. And my older brother couldn't stay in Opole, because there were-- she couldn't feed two people. So he went, he went two towns further, to Staszow. In Staszow, my father had a uncle. My father comes from family that they were slaughterers, shochetim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Slaughterers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So a shochet, a town didn't need more than one or two shochetim. And they all were large families. So they spread around all over Congress Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spread all over--

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland. Congress.

speaker: interviewer
text: Congress. Congress?

speaker: interviewee
text: Congress Poland was the area where the Russians had occupied before World War I. There was Congress Poland. And there was Galicia. Krakow, where the Franz Joseph was was Galicia. And he went to Staszow. I had no patience to stay in Opole.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ooh! [BACKGROUND NOISE] It's only water. OK. You had no patience to stay in Opole.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to know what's happening with my family. I was told, if I go like four or five more towns to Nowy Sacz, [POLISH], [? Neustadt ?], New Town.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? INT: Neustadt, ?] New Town, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were near the Vistula.

speaker: interviewer
text: Vistula.

speaker: interviewee
text: The river. And by train, it was very hard to go, because they check you, the police, the Polish police check you. So there was a boat going from [? Neustadt ?] to Krakow twice a week. And if you work on the ship, they will take your for working. And I went down there. And the captain from the ship said to me, the last time-- trip I took some Jews. I was caught. And I had a lot of trouble. I cannot take you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stayed there. Fact is for first few weeks in Se-- in the end of August and September, I was-- I went to a farmer. And I was feeding his cows, like a shepherd boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was in September, Rosh Hashanah. And I wouldn't stay in the farm for the holiday, so I went into the city to pray in the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Neustadt. And then I went between the Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, back to the farmer. So the farmer said, if you're that religious of a Jew, I'm afraid to keep you. But Yom Kippur I would anyway and not stay with him. So I went back on Yom Kippur. A few days later-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the meantime, while all those months were going by, they start taking the people away from Miedzyrzec. And they took-- they start taking them I don't know where, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean your parents. You're talking about your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my parents couldn't stand there, because there was over-- no food. So my father got, got dressed up like a, a peasant.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know about this? You were not there. How did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: They, they, they told me. I'm going to tell you. My father went to his brother to Ozarow. And my mother put on a handkerchief like a peasant girl, with five children went to the ghetto Krakow. She went by train. She bought tickets on the train. She said that she was a refugee from Poznan. And she lost her husband in the war. And my father had some papers. I don't think my mother had any papers. But when she came to the ghetto-- we had a central, like a post place. My uncle Isaac Levenstein, his wife Sally, and two children lived in the ghetto. So we wrote to them, and they wrote, so that's how we communicate with each other. But my mother couldn't go in to the ghetto. They wouldn't let her in with five children. So she went by horse and wagon to a town near Krakow. It was called Proszowice. In Proszowice, we had-- my father had a very good friend. And the friend took her outskirt from Proszowice, Stogniowice. There was a farm. And he rented an apartment, a one-room apartment, for my mother and the five children. And the mother wrote-- my mother wrote to her brother in ghetto that she lives near Proszowice. And my father left Ozarow. From Ozarow, he went to Strasshof and picked up my brother. Then he came to [NON-ENGLISH], Neustadt, and he picked me up. I was by a family [? Strasburg ?]. The boys I went to cheder, to Hebrew school together. And I wrote that I am there. And we all marched by foot towards Proszowice. One night, again we were afraid to go in at night to Proszowice, so we slept by a farmer. My father had a few zlotys. He paid the farmer. And he gave us some food, and we slept. And we walked down past a house. And that house was covered with a sheet. And little did we know that in this house my mother with five children lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: We went all the way to Proszowice. And the guy in Proszowice said, are you coming from your house? My father said, where, where's my wife and children? He said, you didn't see them? You go in this direction. He said, we passed by. We didn't see them. So he took us by the arms and we walked back a few miles. And we came into the house. And there was my mother, five children, then my father, and my brother, and myself. We were a family from maybe September, maybe beginning of October in 1941. We were all together.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the end of '41, they wanted to take my father into a labor camp. They said, for you to get the rationing, some of you people have to go to work for the Germans, to Krakow. To save our father, we said we're going to send my older brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my older brother went. And he was taken to Krakow in a camp that Miller was the Lagerfuhrer. It was called [? Allach. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: In March they came again. And they said, you still get eight rationing. You're a large family. And because we didn't know nobody, maybe there was protectzia. They said, we need another one to go to the brick factory. So I went. We were five months together, but it was the toughest thing in my life to remember, when there was nothing to cook. I did-- and my mother would cook a few potatoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we would say to her-- she would give everybody a little bit. And we'd say, mother, why don't you eat? She said, I'm not hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] The pain of our mother, that, uh, she has to see her children being hungry. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What's up?
